content:
# Exodus Chapter 26 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~48r–49r (PDF pp. 93–95); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 68–69 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_026_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_026_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_026_page_003.jpg`

---

### The Inner Curtains — Woven Fabric for the Dwelling (vv. 1–6)

**1** "Make the dwelling place itself out of ten curtains of finely spun linen, with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn. Have cherubim skillfully worked into them. **2** Each curtain is to be twenty-eight cubits long and four cubits wide — all the curtains are to be the same size. **3** Join five of the curtains to one another, and the other five curtains to one another. **4** Make loops of blue yarn along the edge of the last curtain in the first set, and do the same along the edge of the last curtain in the second set. **5** Make fifty loops on one curtain and fifty loops on the matching curtain of the second set, with the loops directly opposite each other. **6** Then make fifty gold clasps and join the curtains to one another with the clasps, so that the dwelling place becomes one unified whole."

---

### The Goat-Hair Covering — A Tent over the Dwelling (vv. 7–14)

**7** "Make curtains of goat hair to serve as a tent over the dwelling place — make eleven of them. **8** Each curtain is to be thirty cubits long and four cubits wide — all eleven curtains are to be the same size. **9** Join five of the curtains into one set and the other six into another set, and fold the sixth curtain double at the front of the tent. **10** Make fifty loops along the edge of the last curtain in the first set and fifty loops along the edge of the last curtain in the second set. **11** Then make fifty bronze clasps, put them through the loops, and join the tent together so that it becomes one piece. **12** As for the extra length that hangs over — the remaining half-curtain — let it hang down over the back of the dwelling place. **13** The extra cubit on each side — the length left over along the sides of the tent curtains — will hang down over the sides of the dwelling place on both sides, covering it completely. **14** Make a covering for the tent out of ram skins dyed red, and over that a covering of fine leather."

---

### The Frames — Standing the Dwelling Upright (vv. 15–25)

**15** "Make upright frames of acacia wood for the dwelling place. **16** Each frame is to be ten cubits tall and a cubit and a half wide. **17** Each frame is to have two tenons fitted to each other. Make all the frames of the dwelling place this way. **18** Make the frames for the dwelling place: twenty frames for the south side, **19** with forty silver bases under the twenty frames — two bases under each frame, one for each tenon. **20** For the second side of the dwelling place, the north side, make twenty frames **21** and their forty silver bases — two bases under each frame. **22** For the far end of the dwelling place, the west side, make six frames. **23** And make two frames for the corners of the dwelling place at the far end. **24** They are to be joined at the bottom and fitted together at the top through a single ring. Both corner frames are to be made this way. **25** So there will be eight frames with their silver bases — sixteen bases in all, two bases under each frame."

---

### The Crossbars — Holding Everything Together (vv. 26–30)

**26** "Make crossbars of acacia wood: five for the frames on one side of the dwelling place, **27** five for the frames on the other side, and five for the frames on the far end, to the west. **28** The central crossbar, running through the middle of the frames, is to extend from one end to the other. **29** Overlay the frames with gold and make gold rings to hold the crossbars. Overlay the crossbars with gold as well. **30** Set up the dwelling place according to the plan you were shown on the mountain."

---

### The Veil — Separating the Holy from the Most Holy (vv. 31–35)

**31** "Make a veil of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely spun linen, with cherubim skillfully worked into it. **32** Hang it on four posts of acacia wood overlaid with gold, standing on four silver bases, with gold hooks. **33** Hang the veil from the clasps, and bring the ark of the testimony behind the veil. The veil will separate the holy place from the most holy place for you. **34** Place the mercy seat on the ark of the testimony in the most holy place. **35** Set the table outside the veil on the north side, and place the lampstand opposite the table on the south side of the dwelling place."

---

### The Entrance Screen — The Doorway into God's House (vv. 36–37)

**36** "For the entrance to the tent, make a screen of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely spun linen — the work of an embroiderer. **37** Make five posts of acacia wood for the screen and overlay them with gold; their hooks are to be gold. And cast five bronze bases for them."

---

## Meaning Notes

**vv. 1–6 — THE DWELLING AS A SINGLE FABRIC (Heb. *ve'et-hammishkan ta'aseh eser yeri'ot shesh moshezar utekheleth ve'argaman vetola'ath shani keruvim ma'aseh choshev ta'aseh otam*, וְאֶת־הַמִּשְׁכָּ֥ן תַּעֲשֶׂ֖ה עֶ֣שֶׂר יְרִיעֹ֑ת שֵׁ֣שׁ מׇשְׁזָ֗ר וּתְכֵ֤לֶת וְאַרְגָּמָן֙ וְתֹלַ֣עַת שָׁנִ֔י כְּרֻבִ֛ים מַעֲשֵׂ֥ה חֹשֵׁ֖ב תַּעֲשֶׂ֥ה אֹתָֽם):** The chapter opens with the *mishkan* (מִשְׁכָּן, "dwelling place") itself — not the furniture inside it (that was ch. 25) but the fabric walls that create the sacred space. The word *mishkan* comes from *shakan* (שָׁכַן, "to dwell, to tent"), the same root behind *shekhinah*. The dwelling is not called a temple (*heikhal*) or a house (*bayith*) but a *mishkan* — a place of dwelling, of settling, of tenting. The structure is, at its core, fabric: ten *yeri'ot* (יְרִיעֹת, "curtains, tent-panels"), each woven from the same materials specified in 25:4 for the offering — *shesh moshezar* (שֵׁשׁ מׇשְׁזָר, "finely twisted/spun linen"), *tekheleth* (תְּכֵלֶת, blue-violet), *argaman* (אַרְגָּמָן, purple), and *tola'ath shani* (תוֹלַעַת שָׁנִי, scarlet).

The critical phrase is *keruvim ma'aseh choshev* (כְּרֻבִים מַעֲשֵׂה חֹשֵׁב, "cherubim, the work of a skilled designer/thinker"). The word *choshev* (חֹשֵׁב) comes from *chashav* (חָשַׁב, "to think, to plan, to reckon, to design") — the artisan who works the cherubim into the fabric is literally a "thinker," a designer whose craft is an act of intellect. This is not simple weaving but *thought made visible in thread*. The cherubim are not appliqued or embroidered onto the surface; they are woven into the very structure of the fabric, integral to its being. Anyone inside the dwelling, looking up, would see cherubim in the ceiling — winged guardians overhead, woven into the sky of God's tent.

The insistence that the ten curtains become *echad* (אֶחָד, "one") through the gold clasps (v. 6: *vehayah hammishkan echad*, וְהָיָ֥ה הַמִּשְׁכָּ֖ן אֶחָֽד, "and the dwelling place shall be one") is theologically resonant. The dwelling must not be ten separate pieces but one unified whole. The clasps (*qerasim*, קְרָסִים, from *qaras*, meaning "hook, clasp") are the hidden joints that make multiplicity into unity. The LXX renders *echad* here as *hen* (ἕν, "one"), preserving the emphasis on the structure's fundamental oneness. Fifty gold clasps — the number of jubilee, of completion and release — bind the sacred space into a single fabric.

**vv. 7–14 — THE GOAT-HAIR TENT: PROTECTION OVER BEAUTY (Heb. *ve'asita yeri'ot izzim le'ohel al-hammishkan*, וְעָשִׂ֙יתָ֙ יְרִיעֹ֣ת עִזִּ֔ים לְאֹ֖הֶל עַל־הַמִּשְׁכָּ֑ן):** Over the linen-and-yarn curtains (the *mishkan* proper) goes a second layer: curtains of goat hair (*izzim*, עִזִּים). This outer layer is called *ohel* (אֹהֶל, "tent"), distinguishing it from the *mishkan* ("dwelling") beneath it. The distinction is precise: the *mishkan* is the beautiful inner fabric with its woven cherubim; the *ohel* is the functional goat-hair covering that protects it from the elements. Beauty is interior; utility is exterior. The most exquisite artistry faces inward, toward the presence, not outward, toward the world.

Goat-hair fabric (*yeri'ot izzim*) is the standard tent material of Bedouin and pastoral cultures across the Near East — dark, coarse, waterproof, and durable. The eleven panels (one more than the inner curtains) provide extra coverage: the sixth curtain is doubled over at the front (*mul penei ha'ohel*, מוּל֙ פְּנֵ֣י הָאֹ֔הֶל, "facing the front of the tent"), and the excess length hangs down over the rear and sides (vv. 12–13). The goat-hair tent wraps the dwelling like a protective cloak, its rough exterior hiding the glory within. From outside, the tabernacle looks like any other desert tent — dark goat hair, unremarkable, portable. Only those who enter discover the blue and purple and gold and the cherubim woven overhead.

The bronze clasps (*qarasei nechoshet*, קַרְסֵ֣י נְחֹ֔שֶׁת) that join the goat-hair panels (v. 11) are a step down from the gold clasps of the inner curtains — the hierarchy of materials maps the hierarchy of holiness: gold within, bronze without. The additional coverings of ram skins dyed red (*orot eilim me'oddamim*, עֹרֹ֨ת אֵילִ֧ם מְאׇדָּמִ֛ים) and fine leather (*orot techashim*, עֹרֹ֥ת תְּחָשִׁ֖ים) form the outermost layers (v. 14), providing final weatherproofing. The structure, from outside to inside, moves from animal skin through goat hair to exquisite linen and yarn — from the raw and weathered to the refined and beautiful, a journey from the ordinary world into the divine presence.

**vv. 15–25 — THE ACACIA FRAMES: STANDING WOOD (Heb. *ve'asita et-haqqerashim lammishkan atsei shittim omedim*, וְעָשִׂ֥יתָ אֶת־הַקְּרָשִׁ֖ים לַמִּשְׁכָּ֑ן עֲצֵ֥י שִׁטִּ֖ים עֹמְדִֽים):** The *qerashim* (קְרָשִׁים, "frames, boards, planks") are the structural skeleton of the dwelling. The word is related to *qaresh* (קֶרֶשׁ), and while traditionally translated "boards," modern scholarship favors "frames" — open rectangular structures rather than solid planks, given the logistics of weight and transport. Each frame stands ten cubits tall and a cubit and a half wide (roughly 15 feet by 2.25 feet), and the participle *omedim* (עֹמְדִים, "standing") emphasizes their vertical orientation — they stand upright, like sentinels.

Each frame rests in two *adanei khasef* (אַדְנֵי כָסֶף, "silver bases/sockets"), and the total count — forty bases for the south side, forty for the north, sixteen for the west, totaling ninety-six silver bases — represents an enormous quantity of silver. Exodus 38:25–27 will later reveal that this silver came from the census tax: each Israelite male contributed a half-shekel, and the total was melted into the bases. The foundation of God's dwelling thus rests literally on the counted identity of every member of the community. Each base represents a specific person, a specific contribution, a specific life. The dwelling does not float on air; it stands on the silver of a whole people.

The two *yadot* (יָדֹת, "tenons, hands") per frame (v. 17) — literally "hands" — are the projections that fit into the silver bases. The image is tactile: the wooden frame reaches down with its two hands into the silver socket, gripping the foundation. The *meshullavot ishah el-achotah* (מְשֻׁלָּבֹת אִשָּׁה אֶל־אֲחֹתָהּ, "joined one to another," literally "a woman to her sister") describes how the tenons interlock, using the feminine form of the idiom for mutual relationship — sister to sister, piece to piece, each member of the structure in relationship with its neighbor.

**vv. 26–30 — THE CROSSBARS: THE HIDDEN SPINE (Heb. *ve'asita verichim atsei shittim*, וְעָשִׂ֥יתָ בְרִיחִ֖ם עֲצֵ֣י שִׁטִּ֑ים):** The *berichim* (בְרִיחִים, "crossbars, bolts") are horizontal beams that run through rings on the frames, binding them together laterally. The word *beriach* (בְּרִיחַ) can mean "bar" or "bolt" — something that passes through to lock and secure. Five crossbars on each side and five on the rear provide structural rigidity to the entire frame assembly. The central bar (*habberiach hattikhon*, הַבְּרִ֥יחַ הַתִּיכֹ֖ן, "the middle crossbar") runs the full length of the wall *min-haqqatseh el-haqqatseh* (מִן־הַקָּצֶ֖ה אֶל־הַקָּצֶֽה, "from end to end"), serving as the spine that holds everything together. What is visible is the gold-covered frames; what holds them together is the hidden bar running through their centers.

Verse 30 returns to the governing principle: *vahaqemota et-hammishkan kemishpato asher hor'etha bahar* (וַהֲקֵמֹתָ֖ אֶת־הַמִּשְׁכָּ֑ן כְּמִ֨שְׁפָּט֔וֹ אֲשֶׁ֥ר הׇרְאֵ֖יתָ בָּהָֽר, "set up the dwelling place according to its plan, as you were shown on the mountain"). The word *mishpat* (מִשְׁפָּט) here means "plan, prescribed manner, regulation" — the same word usually translated "justice" or "judgment." The architectural plan is a *mishpat* — a right ordering, a just arrangement. The way the dwelling is assembled is not arbitrary but reflective of a divine order, a heavenly justice built into the very structure of sacred space.

**vv. 31–35 — THE VEIL: THE BOUNDARY OF THE HOLY (Heb. *ve'asita farokhet tekheleth ve'argaman vetola'ath shani veshesh moshezar ma'aseh choshev ya'aseh otah keruvim*, וְעָשִׂ֣יתָ פָרֹ֗כֶת תְּכֵ֧לֶת וְאַרְגָּמָ֛ן וְתוֹלַ֥עַת שָׁנִ֖י וְשֵׁ֣שׁ מׇשְׁזָ֑ר מַעֲשֵׂ֥ה חֹשֵׁ֖ב יַעֲשֶׂ֥ה אֹתָ֛הּ כְּרֻבִֽים):** The *parokhet* (פָּרֹכֶת, "veil, curtain of separation") is the most theologically charged piece of fabric in the entire structure. This single curtain divides the interior of the dwelling into two rooms: the *qodesh* (קֹדֶשׁ, "holy place") and the *qodesh haqqodashim* (קֹדֶשׁ הַקֳּדָשִׁים, "holy of holies, most holy place"). The word *parokhet* appears to derive from a root meaning "to separate, to screen, to block" — it is a barrier, a boundary, a membrane between the accessible and the inaccessible.

The veil is made of the same materials as the inner curtains — blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen — and like them, it bears cherubim woven by the *choshev* ("thinker-designer"). The veil is, in effect, a concentrated version of the ceiling: the same artistry, the same guardian figures, now compressed into a single hanging curtain. The cherubim on the veil guard the entrance to the most holy place, just as the cherubim of Genesis 3:24 guard the way back to Eden. The tabernacle's innermost room, behind the veil, is a symbolic return to the garden — the place where God dwells, where humanity was meant to live, now accessible only through the atoning rituals performed once a year.

The veil hangs from the gold clasps (*haqqerasim*, הַקְּרָסִים) that join the two halves of the inner curtains (v. 33), placing it precisely at the junction — the seam — of the dwelling. The most sacred boundary in Israelite worship coincides with the structural joint of the building. Where the fabric is clasped together, the veil descends. The LXX renders *parokhet* as *katapetasma* (καταπέτασμα, "that which is spread out, a veil"), the same word the Synoptic Gospels will use when describing the veil of the temple that was "torn in two from top to bottom" at the death of Jesus (Matt. 27:51; Mark 15:38; Luke 23:45). What Exodus builds, the Gospels will open.

The spatial arrangement described in verse 35 creates the liturgical geography of the holy place: the table of the bread of the Presence on the north side, the lampstand on the south side, facing each other across the room. Light falls on bread. The two furnishings are in relationship — the lamp illuminates the table, the bread is visible by the lampstand's light. The room of ordinary holiness is a lit dining room, a space of warmth and provision, while behind the veil, in darkness and silence, stands the ark with its mercy seat and the cherubim — the place of encounter, unseen and unapproachable except by the blood of atonement.

**vv. 36–37 — THE ENTRANCE SCREEN: THRESHOLD OF ENCOUNTER (Heb. *ve'asita masakh lefethach ha'ohel tekheleth ve'argaman vetola'ath shani veshesh moshezar ma'aseh roqem*, וְעָשִׂ֤יתָ מָסָךְ֙ לְפֶ֣תַח הָאֹ֔הֶל תְּכֵ֧לֶת וְאַרְגָּמָ֛ן וְתוֹלַ֥עַת שָׁנִ֖י וְשֵׁ֣שׁ מׇשְׁזָ֑ר מַעֲשֵׂ֥ה רֹקֵֽם):** The *masakh* (מָסָךְ, "screen, covering") at the entrance is distinguished from the *parokhet* in two ways. First, it is the work of a *roqem* (רֹקֵם, "embroiderer") rather than a *choshev* ("skilled designer/thinker") — a lower grade of artistry. Second, it has no cherubim. The entrance screen marks the threshold between the courtyard and the holy place; the veil marks the threshold between the holy place and the most holy place. Each boundary is more restrictive, more exclusive, more artistically intense than the one before it.

The five posts (*ammudehem chamishah*, עַמּוּדֵיהֶ֖ם חֲמִשָּׁ֑ה) stand on bronze bases (*adnei nechoshet*, אַדְנֵ֥י נְחֹֽשֶׁת), whereas the veil's four posts stand on silver bases. The material hierarchy is consistent: the closer to the most holy place, the more precious the metal. Bronze at the entrance, silver at the veil, gold on the ark. The tabernacle's architecture is a graded approach to the divine presence — each step inward is a step upward in holiness, in costliness, in the weight of sacred meaning.

---

## Translator's Reflection

**A God who lives in fabric — the architecture of nearness:**

Exodus 26 is a chapter of measurements, materials, and construction details. It is easily the kind of text a reader skips — curtain dimensions, clasp counts, frame specifications. And yet this chapter answers one of the most profound questions in the Bible: what does it look like when the infinite God moves into the neighborhood?

**1. The dwelling is fabric, not stone.** The most striking thing about the tabernacle is what it is not. It is not a ziggurat. It is not a stone temple. It is not a permanent edifice anchored to the earth. It is fabric — linen and goat hair and animal skins, draped over a wooden frame, held together by clasps and crossbars. It can be taken down in a morning, loaded onto carts, carried through the wilderness, and set up again by evening. The God of Sinai, who spoke from fire and earthquake, chooses to live in a tent. The impermanence is deliberate. God does not anchor himself to a place; he anchors himself to a people. When they move, he moves. When they stop, he stops. The fabric dwelling says: my commitment is to you, not to this location. I will be as portable as you are, as vulnerable to wind and weather as you are, as temporary in any given campsite as you are. The God of the cosmos becomes a nomad.

**2. The layers — from rough to radiant.** The tabernacle's architecture is a study in layers, and the layers move from the ordinary to the extraordinary as one moves inward. The outermost surface is animal skin — weathered, functional, unremarkable. Beneath that: goat hair, dark and coarse, the fabric of any Bedouin tent. Beneath that: the inner curtains, a riot of color — blue and purple and scarlet, woven with cherubim, joined by gold clasps. The beauty is hidden. The glory is interior. From outside, the tabernacle is just a tent. From inside, it is heaven. This is a theology of hiddenness: God's most extraordinary presence looks, from the outside, utterly ordinary. The divine glory does not advertise itself. It must be entered to be known.

**3. The veil — the necessary boundary.** The *parokhet* is a single curtain, but it carries the weight of the entire biblical story. It separates the holy from the most holy — the place where priests serve daily from the place where only the high priest enters, only once a year, only with blood. The veil says: you may come close, but not all the way. There is a limit. The God who wants nearness also maintains distance — not out of indifference but out of the sheer danger of unmediated holiness. The veil protects the people from a glory that would consume them. It is not a wall of rejection but a membrane of mercy — it permits proximity while preventing destruction. The cherubim woven into it recall Eden's guardians: the way back to God's immediate presence is blocked, but not permanently. The veil exists to be approached, to be respected, and — in the fullness of time — to be torn.

**4. The gold clasps at the center.** A small detail with large implications: the veil hangs precisely where the gold clasps join the two halves of the inner curtains (v. 33). The most sacred boundary in Israel's worship — the division between holy and most holy — coincides with the point where the dwelling's two fabric halves become one. The seam and the separation occupy the same spot. The clasps that unite are also the line that divides. This is the paradox of sacred space: the place of joining is the place of division. The same point that makes the dwelling *echad* ("one") is the point that splits it into two rooms. Unity and separation coexist at the center. The God who dwells in the innermost room is both joined to his people (the fabric is one) and separated from them (the veil descends). The clasps hold both truths together.

**5. The furniture arranges a theology.** Verse 35 is easily overlooked, but it contains the liturgical map of the holy place: table on the north, lampstand on the south, facing each other. The priest who enters the holy place walks between bread and light — provision on one side, illumination on the other. Behind him, the entrance screen. Before him, the veil. He serves in a space defined by the most basic elements of domestic life — food and light — and bounded by the most profound theological realities — access and restriction, invitation and limit. The holy place is simultaneously a home and a threshold. It is comfortable enough to work in and awesome enough to tremble in. The bread says: God nourishes. The lamp says: God illuminates. The veil says: God is holy. The arrangement is not accidental; it is a spatial theology, a room that teaches by its very layout.

**6. The infinite in a tent — how God makes the abstract concrete.** The deepest question of Exodus 26 is the question posed by the chapter's content itself: how does the God who fills heaven and earth dwell in a structure forty-five feet long and fifteen feet wide? The answer the text gives is not philosophical but architectural. God does not explain how he can be simultaneously transcendent and local; he simply gives the blueprints. The measurements, the materials, the clasps and frames and crossbars — these are the answer. The infinite becomes local not through a theory of divine presence but through acacia wood and twisted linen and gold-covered frames standing in silver sockets. The abstract becomes concrete not through explanation but through construction. "Make it," God says. "Make it according to the pattern I showed you on the mountain." The tabernacle does not resolve the paradox of divine immanence and transcendence; it inhabits the paradox. It is a tent that contains what the heavens cannot contain. It is fabric that houses fire. It is a portable structure that carries the weight of the eternal. And it works — not because the theology makes sense on paper, but because God said to build it, and then showed up.

The pattern was shown on the mountain. The materials came from willing hearts. The fabric was woven by thinkers and embroiderers. And when it was finished, the glory of YHWH filled the dwelling, and Moses could not enter the tent of meeting because the cloud had settled on it (40:34–35). The chapter of measurements and clasps and frame specifications ends, ultimately, in a cloud — in a presence so thick and real that the greatest prophet in Israel's history must wait outside the door of the tent he built. The architecture served its purpose. The dwelling became what its name promised: a place where God *dwells*.

— *PNEUMA*
